questions.q: Explain the concept of soil formation and the factors that influence it. Describe the role of weathering in soil formation.
questions.answer: Soil formation refers to the process by which soil is created through the weathering of rocks and the decomposition of organic matter. The factors that influence soil formation include climate, topography, vegetation, and parent material. Weathering plays a crucial role in soil formation as it breaks down rocks into smaller particles, releasing essential nutrients and minerals. There are three types of weathering: mechanical, chemical, and biological. Mechanical weathering involves the physical disintegration of rocks, while chemical weathering involves the chemical alteration of rocks. Biological weathering involves the breakdown of rocks by living organisms.
marking_scheme: Description of factors influencing soil formation (1 mark)
Role of weathering in soil formation (1 mark)

questions.q: What is soil erosion? Describe the types of soil erosion and their causes. Explain the consequences of soil erosion.
questions.answer: Soil erosion refers to the removal of soil particles from one location to another through natural or human-induced processes. There are three main types of soil erosion: sheet erosion, rill erosion, and gully erosion. Sheet erosion occurs when a uniform layer of soil is removed from a large area, while rill erosion occurs when small channels or rills are formed on the soil surface. Gully erosion occurs when large channels or gullies are formed on the soil surface. The causes of soil erosion include deforestation, overgrazing, and construction activities. The consequences of soil erosion include loss of fertile land, increased sedimentation in waterways, and decreased water quality.

questions.q: Discuss the importance of soil conservation. Describe the methods of soil conservation and their benefits.
questions.answer: Soil conservation is essential to maintain soil health, prevent soil erosion, and ensure sustainable agriculture. The methods of soil conservation include contour farming, strip cropping, and terracing. Contour farming involves planting crops across a slope to reduce soil erosion, while strip cropping involves planting crops in alternate strips to reduce soil erosion. Terracing involves creating flat plots of land on slopes to reduce soil erosion. The benefits of soil conservation include increased crop yields, improved water quality, and reduced sedimentation in waterways.
marking_scheme: Examples of soil conservation practices (1 mark)
Role of government and community in soil conservation (1 mark)

questions.q: A farmer has 2000 kg of fertilizer to apply to his field. If the recommended application rate is 50 kg/ha, how many hectares can the farmer fertilize?
questions.answer:
Calculation:
1. Determine the total amount of fertilizer available: 2000 kg
2. Determine the recommended application rate: 50 kg/ha
3. Calculate the number of hectares that can be fertilized: 2000 kg / 50 kg/ha = 40 ha

questions.q: A soil sample has a pH of 6.5. If the pH is increased by 1 unit, what will be the new pH?
questions.answer:
Calculation:
1. Determine the initial pH: 6.5
2. Determine the change in pH: +1 unit
3. Calculate the new pH: 6.5 + 1 = 7.5

questions.q: A soil has a water-holding capacity of 30%. If the soil has a bulk density of 1.2 g/cm³, how much water can be held in 1 m³ of soil?
questions.answer:
Calculation:
1. Determine the bulk density of the soil: 1.2 g/cm³
2. Convert the bulk density to kg/m³: 1.2 g/cm³ x (1 kg/1000 g) x (1,000,000 cm³/1 m³) = 1200 kg/m³
3. Determine the water-holding capacity: 30%
4. Calculate the amount of water that can be held: 1200 kg/m³ x 0.3 = 360 kg/m³
5. Convert the amount of water to liters: 360 kg/m³ x (1 L/1 kg) = 360 L/m³
